title: Your Body Is Good and It Will Change

required.title: [Read Aloud] The Wonderful Way Babies Are Made
required.author: Larry Christenson

required.title: [Read Aloud / Reference] It's Not the Stork!
required.author: Robie Harris

suggested.title: [Read Aloud] Amazing You! Getting Smart About Your Private Parts
suggested.author: Gail Saltz

suggested.title: [Reference] Anatomical diagrams appropriate for the student's age

competence_markers.description: The child uses correct anatomical terminology without embarrassment
The child can explain reproduction at a basic level using accurate vocabulary
The child understands that bodies change during puberty and is not anxious about it
The child can articulate body boundary rules clearly and knows what to do if boundaries are violated
The child understands the biological differences between boys and girls as simple, observable facts
The child is comfortable asking questions about the body to trusted adults

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me the correct names for the parts of the body we talked about.
narration_prompts.detail: Why is it important to use the real names?
Tell me what you know, using the real words.

narration_prompts.prompt: What are some ways that boys' and girls' bodies are different?
narration_prompts.detail: Why are these differences nothing to be embarrassed about?
What do you know about it so far? What questions do you have?

narration_prompts.prompt: What are the rules about your body and other people?
narration_prompts.detail: What should you do if someone makes you uncomfortable?

cross_connections.description: The human body as a biological system is the broader context for this packet's specific focus on reproductive anatomy and development.
The maturity to discuss these topics appropriately and the self-possession to maintain body boundaries are IC1 capacities.
The biological differences introduced here are developed in 4.4.3's exploration of what it means to be a boy or a girl.
Body boundaries and self-possession are the earliest foundations of the sexual integrity developed in 4.4.5.

content.why_this_matters:
Your body is an amazing thing. It can run, climb, swim, heal itself when you get a cut, fight off sickness, and grow bigger every year. You have bones that hold you up, muscles that move you, a brain that thinks, a heart that pumps blood through miles of blood vessels, and lungs that bring in the air you need to live. Your body is not something to be embarrassed about. It is something to understand.

Right now, your body is a child's body. That is exactly what it should be. But it will not stay this way. Over the next several years, your body will change in some big ways — ways that are completely normal, completely expected, and nothing to be afraid of. Everyone goes through these changes. Your parents went through them. Your grandparents went through them. Every adult you know went through them.

This packet teaches you the truth about your body — how it works, how babies are made, what the correct names for your body parts are, and what your rights are when it comes to your own body. You will learn these things here, from people who care about you, using real words — not baby words, not embarrassed words, and not from confusing things you might hear from other kids or see on a screen.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Correct anatomical vocabulary** — the real names for body parts, used without embarrassment
2. **Basic understanding of reproduction** — where babies come from, stated simply and honestly
3. **Awareness that bodies change** — the concept that puberty is coming and is normal
4. **Body boundaries and personal safety** — the child's body belongs to them, and they have the right and responsibility to protect it
5. **The difference between boys and girls** — stated as biological fact, without anxiety or political complication
6. **Comfort with the topic** — the ability to ask questions about the body without shame
content.core_content:
### Part 1: The Real Names for Your Body Parts

Every part of your body has a real name. You know some of them already — arm, leg, nose, stomach, elbow, knee. Some parts of your body are private — they are covered by your underwear or bathing suit — and these parts have real names too. Here they are:

**Boys have:**
- A **penis** — the organ on the outside of the body used for urination and, when a man is grown, for reproduction
- **Testicles** — two small organs inside a pouch of skin called the **scrotum**, located below the penis. Testicles produce the cells that are needed for making a baby.

**Girls have:**
- A **vulva** — the outside parts of the female reproductive area
- A **vagina** — the opening that leads to the inside of the body, to the **uterus** (sometimes called the womb), which is where a baby grows
- **Breasts** — which will develop during puberty and can produce milk to feed a baby

These are not bad words. They are not embarrassing words. They are the same kind of words as "elbow" or "knee" — they are the correct names for parts of your body. Using the right words is important because it means you can talk clearly about your body with your parents, your doctor, or any trusted adult. If something ever hurts or does not feel right, you need to be able to say exactly what you mean.

### Part 2: Where Babies Come From

Babies do not come from storks or from a cabbage patch. Babies are made from two tiny cells — one from a man and one from a woman.

The cell from the man is called a **sperm**. It is incredibly small — so small you cannot see it without a microscope. The cell from the woman is called an **egg** (or **ovum**). It is also very small, but it is the largest single cell in the human body.

When a sperm and an egg join together, a new life begins. This joined cell is called a **fertilized egg**, and it attaches to the wall of the mother's **uterus** — a special organ inside her body that is designed to protect and nourish a growing baby. Over the course of about nine months, that single cell divides and grows into a complete human being — with a brain, a heart, fingers, toes, eyes, and everything else.

When the baby is ready to be born, the mother's body goes through a process called **labor**, and the baby is born — usually through the vagina, sometimes through a surgical procedure called a **cesarean section** (or C-section) if the baby cannot be delivered safely the other way.

This is how every person who has ever lived came into the world — including you. It is one of the most remarkable processes in nature, and there is nothing embarrassing about it.

As you get older, you will learn more details about how the sperm and egg come together, how the baby develops, and what the mother's body goes through. For now, the important thing is: you know the basics, and you know the real words.

### Part 3: Bodies Are Different — And That Is Good

Boys and girls are built differently. You can see some of these differences, and some are inside the body where you cannot see them.

Boys tend to be built for strength and speed in certain ways. Their bodies will eventually produce a hormone called **testosterone** that will make their muscles bigger, their voices deeper, and their bodies hairier. Girls tend to be built for endurance and flexibility in certain ways. Their bodies will eventually produce hormones called **estrogen** and **progesterone** that will prepare their bodies for the possibility of carrying a baby someday.

These differences are real. They are not something to argue about — they are biology. They do not mean boys are better than girls or girls are better than boys. They mean boys and girls are different, and those differences are part of what makes human beings work. You will learn much more about this in 4.4.3.

### Part 4: Your Body Will Change

Right now, your body is a child's body. Sometime in the next few years — for some children earlier, for some later — your body will begin to change in ways that are noticeable and sometimes surprising. This process is called **puberty**, and it happens to everyone.

You do not need to know all the details right now. What you need to know is this:

- **Puberty is normal.** Every adult you know went through it.
- **It happens at different times for different people.** Some children start puberty earlier, some later. Both are normal.
- **Your body will grow bigger, and its shape will change.** You will get taller. Your body will look more like an adult's body than a child's body.
- **You might feel different emotions.** Hormones — the chemicals that cause puberty — also affect how you feel. You might feel things more intensely than before. This is normal.
- **We will talk about it more as it gets closer.** You will not be surprised. You will know what is happening and why. You can ask any question, any time, and you will get an honest answer.

### Part 5: Your Body Belongs to You

This is one of the most important things you will ever learn: **your body belongs to you.** No one has the right to touch you in ways that make you uncomfortable, and no one has the right to ask you to touch them in ways that feel wrong.

Here are the rules:

**Your private parts are private.** The parts of your body covered by your underwear or bathing suit are not for other people to see or touch — except for specific, appropriate reasons like a doctor's examination (with a parent present) or a parent helping a very young child with bathing or toileting.

**If someone touches you in a way that feels wrong, it is not your fault.** It does not matter who the person is — even if it is an adult you know and trust, even if it is a family member. If the touch feels wrong, it is wrong. You did not cause it. You are not in trouble.

**Tell a trusted adult immediately.** If someone touches your private parts, asks you to touch theirs, asks you to keep a secret about touching, shows you pictures of naked people, or makes you feel uncomfortable in any way related to your body — tell your parent, your mentor, or another trusted adult right away. Tell them even if the person told you not to. Tell them even if you are scared. Tell them even if you think no one will believe you. Keep telling until someone listens.

**You have the right to say no.** Even to hugs or kisses from relatives if you do not want them. Your body is yours. You decide who touches it and how.

These rules are not about fear. The world is mostly full of good people who would never hurt you. But knowing these rules means you are prepared — and preparation is not the same as fear. It is wisdom.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Anatomical Vocabulary
**Developing well:** The child uses correct terms (penis, vagina, vulva, testicles, uterus, breasts) naturally and without giggling or embarrassment. They may self-correct from slang to correct terms.

**Developing slowly:** The child avoids correct terminology, substitutes euphemisms, or giggles persistently. They may have absorbed the message from other sources that these words are "bad" or "funny."

**Diagnostics:** Giggling is developmentally normal in the early stages. It usually subsides with repeated, matter-of-fact use. If it persists, examine whether the child is getting contradictory messages from other adults or peers about whether these words are appropriate.

### 2. Basic Reproductive Understanding
**Developing well:** The child can explain that a baby grows from the combination of a cell from a man and a cell from a woman, inside the mother's body. They may add details from their questions and your answers.

**Developing slowly:** The child cannot articulate the basics of reproduction or reverts to myths (storks, cabbage patches). This may indicate that the material was not presented clearly or that the child was not developmentally ready and needs the material revisited.

**Diagnostics:** Revisit the material simply and without pressure. Some children need multiple exposures before the information integrates. This is normal.

### 3. Body Boundary Clarity
**Developing well:** The child can state the rules clearly: private parts are private, tell a trusted adult if someone makes you uncomfortable, it is never your fault, you have the right to say no. They demonstrate this understanding in practice — for example, declining unwanted physical affection from relatives.

**Developing slowly:** The child cannot articulate the rules or does not seem to internalize them. They may be overly compliant with physical affection they do not want, or they may not understand the concept of private parts.

**Diagnostics:** If the child cannot internalize body boundary rules, investigate gently whether something is interfering. Revisit the material at shorter intervals. Ensure other adults in the child's life are reinforcing the same message.

### 4. Comfort with the Topic
**Developing well:** The child asks questions about their body and about reproduction openly and without visible shame. They treat it as interesting rather than taboo.

**Developing slowly:** The child shuts down when body topics arise, changes the subject, or becomes visibly anxious. They do not ask questions.

**Diagnostics:** Has someone communicated shame about bodies to this child? Have they been scolded for curiosity about their body? Your job is to counter that message by being consistently open, calm, and welcoming of questions.
